FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), 4.0 data release, 11,875 children recruited from 21 U.S. sites, baseline ages 9-10. Analytic sample N=10,258.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional secondary data analysis using modified Poisson regression to examine cyberbullying and eating disorder symptoms.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in International Journal of Eating Disorders (2023).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The only exposure variables are cyberbullying victimization and cyberbullying perpetration (assessed via online/texts/social media items). No separate numeric estimate for any qualifying phone/SM variable (time on phone, time on SM, named platform, texting, app use, etc.) appears anywhere in the paper including Table 2 or supplementary materials. Step 2: Not applicable as the exposure is cyberbullying, not a composite screen time measure. Binding clarification: cyberbullying alone fails FT-IC2.

notes: Paper focuses exclusively on cyberbullying as IV and eating disorder symptoms as DV. Cyberbullying alone (without a separately-measured phone/SM variable having its own coefficient) fails IC2 per binding clarification.

FT_IC1.evidence: The sample is 645 Dutch adolescents from the Amsterdam Born Children and their Development (ABCD) cohort study (Van Eijsden et al., 2010), a Dutch longitudinal cohort started in 2003-2004. This is NOT the U.S. NIH-funded Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study. The abstract explicitly states 'Amsterdam Born Children and their Development cohort' and the methods confirm recruitment in Amsterdam, The Netherlands. This is the Dutch ABCD, which is explicitly excluded per FT-IC1.

notes: Dutch ABCD cohort (Amsterdam Born Children and their Development), not U.S. ABCD Study. The paper acknowledges ABCD study participants in acknowledgments but this refers to the Dutch cohort, not the U.S. NIH study.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 4.0 release, longitudinal cohort (baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-10), 21 U.S. recruitment sites. Analytic sample N=8,085.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using linear regression models on ABCD data from baseline through Year 2 follow-up, examining ACEs and bullying victimization in relation to internalizing and externalizing problems.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article published in Journal of Youth and Adolescence (2024).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: The paper examines ACEs and bullying victimization (composite combining cyberbullying + overt + relational + reputational bullying) as predictors of internalizing/externalizing problems. No separate numeric estimate for any qualifying phone/SM-specific variable (time on phone, time on SM, named platform, texting, etc.) appears anywhere in the paper including Tables 2-3 or Supplementary 1-2. The cyberbullying item is embedded in the composite bullying score with no standalone coefficient reported. Step 2: Not a screen-time composite; the composite is multi-modality bullying (cyberbullying + in-person forms), not exclusively phone/SM. Binding clarification: cyberbullying alone fails IC2, and here cyberbullying is only one component of a broader bullying composite with no breakout estimate.

notes: Cyberbullying is measured as a single item contributing to a composite bullying victimization score; no standalone phone/SM coefficient is reported anywhere. The exposure is ACEs and composite bullying, not a qualifying smartphone/SM variable.
